skill.name: matchms
skill.description: Process, clean, and compare mass spectrometry (MS/MS) spectra with Matchms; use when you need reproducible spectral filtering and similarity scoring for metabolomics workflows.
skill.summary: Clean, filter, and compare MS/MS spectra using the Matchms library for reproducible metabolomics data analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 46 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections match non-code text in markdown documentation and audit JSON field values. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are markdown code formatting backticks around Python function names, file paths, and version references, not actual shell command injection. The 'system reconnaissance' detections are ordinary audit report text. The 'hardcoded URL' is a legitimate source repository citation. The skill is a legitimate mass spectrometry data processing tool with a simple Python script and documentation files. No malicious intent, obfuscated code, data exfiltration, or command injection vectors were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' in non-code files. All instances are markdown text, YAML metadata, or audit JSON field values. No actual cryptographic operations exist in this skill. For example, line 72 of matchms_audit_result_v1.json contains the word 'security' in a score note, and line 3 of references/filtering.md describes the Matchms filtering library.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution in Markdown
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged backtick-enclosed text as Ruby shell execution. All instances are markdown code formatting for Python function names (normalize_intensities, default_filters, add_precursor_mz), file paths (scripts/similarity_pipeline.py), package names (matchms, numpy, scipy), and Python code blocks. No actual shell backtick execution is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance in Audit Report
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged text in matchms_audit_result_v1.json as 'system reconnaissance'. The flagged lines contain ordinary evaluation descriptions, such as 'The legacy audit did not record a code-usability failure in the packaged analysis path.' No actual system reconnaissance activity exists in this skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged the GitHub source repository URL in SKILL.md line 7 as a hardcoded URL. This is a legitimate source citation pointing to the skill's upstream repository. No network connections are made by the skill code itself.

content.user_title: Process and Compare Mass Spectra with Matchms
content.value_statement: Mass spectrometry data requires specialized tools for reproducible processing and comparison. This skill uses the Matchms library to clean, filter, and score MS/MS spectra with validated similarity algorithms for metabolomics workflows.
actual_capabilities: Import MS/MS spectra from MGF, MSP, mzML, mzXML, and JSON formats using Matchms readers
Clean and filter spectra with intensity normalization, metadata standardization, and noise removal filters
Compute spectral similarity using Cosine Greedy and Modified Cosine algorithms for library matching
Build reproducible processing pipelines with configurable filter chains and documented parameters
Compare spectra all-vs-all or query-vs-library and inspect score outputs
Execute similarity analysis through a packaged Python script with CLI argument support
limitations: Requires Python 3.10+ and the matchms package to be installed in the execution environment
Cannot process spectra from unsupported file formats without additional format converters
Similarity score quality depends on input data completeness and parameter configuration
Molecular fingerprint similarity requires the optional RDKit dependency

use_cases.title: Metabolomics Spectral Data Processing
use_cases.description: Clean and standardize MS/MS spectra from community formats for downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Spectral Library Matching
use_cases.description: Compare query spectra against a reference library using cosine or modified cosine similarity.

use_cases.title: Reproducible Analysis Pipelines
use_cases.description: Build automated, configurable processing workflows for batch spectral comparison projects.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Spectral Loading
prompt_templates.prompt: Load the spectra from my MGF file at spectra/data.mgf using Matchms and tell me how many spectra were loaded.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user loading a spectra file

prompt_templates.title: Filter and Compare Spectra
prompt_templates.prompt: Load spectra from input.mgf, apply default filters for intensity normalization and peak cleaning, then compute pairwise cosine similarity scores between all spectra.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user performing standard spectral comparison

prompt_templates.title: Build a Custom Processing Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Matchms processing pipeline that loads spectra from queries.mgf and references.msp, applies metadata standardization and intensity normalization filters, then computes modified cosine similarity scores and saves results to a JSON file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user building a reusable workflow

prompt_templates.title: Configure and Tune Similarity Parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Matchms similarity comparison with custom tolerance parameters: use a 0.05 Da m/z tolerance for Cosine Greedy matching, enable precursor mass shift handling for Modified Cosine, and output scores filtered above a 0.7 threshold.

output_examples.input: Show me how to load an MGF file and inspect spectral metadata.
output_examples.output: Loaded 42 spectra from data.mgf. Each spectrum contains peak m/z arrays, intensity arrays, and metadata fields including precursor_mz, charge, and compound_name. The first spectrum has 156 peaks with precursor m/z 452.3 and charge +1.

output_examples.input: Compare spectra from input.mgf using Modified Cosine similarity.
output_examples.output: Calculated pairwise Modified Cosine scores for 42 spectra. Top 3 matches: Spectrum 5 vs Spectrum 12 scored 0.94 with precursor shift of 2.01 Da. Spectrum 3 vs Spectrum 28 scored 0.88 with precursor shift of 1.05 Da. Spectrum 7 vs Spectrum 7 scored 1.00 (self-match).

best_practices: Always validate input file formats and paths before running the similarity pipeline script.
Document all parameters such as m/z tolerance, normalization method, and similarity metric alongside results for reproducibility.
Start with default filters (normalize_intensities, default_filters) before customizing the processing chain.
anti_patterns: Running the similarity pipeline without confirming input file paths or output directory permissions.
Ignoring spectral quality warnings or applying filters in an incorrect order that discards useful data.
Using default similarity parameters without considering domain-specific requirements like precursor mass tolerance.

faq.question: What file formats does Matchms support for spectral import?
faq.answer: Matchms supports MGF, MSP, mzML, mzXML, and JSON formats depending on installed reader packages.

faq.question: What is the difference between Cosine Greedy and Modified Cosine?
faq.answer: Cosine Greedy matches peaks within an m/z tolerance iteratively. Modified Cosine extends this by allowing a precursor mass shift to improve matching for related compounds and adducts.

faq.question: Does this skill require RDKit to be installed?
faq.answer: No. RDKit is optional and only needed for molecular fingerprint-based similarity features.

faq.question: How do I install the matchms package?
faq.answer: Install matchms using pip: pip install matchms. Pin a version range in your project, for example matchms>=0.20,<1.0.

faq.question: Can I compare spectra from different mass spectrometry instruments?
faq.answer: Yes. Matchms normalizes and standardizes metadata across formats, enabling cross-instrument comparison if the spectra share compatible metadata fields.

faq.question: How do I interpret cosine similarity scores?
faq.answer: Scores range from 0 (no similarity) to 1 (identical spectra). Scores above 0.7 generally indicate strong similarity. Threshold depends on your data quality and application.
